name: YugabyteDB
what_it_does: Distributed SQL database compatible with PostgreSQL and Cassandra query languages, providing horizontal scalability with ACID transactions across multiple nodes and regions.
use_cases: Horizontally scalable Postgres-compatible database for agent state stores that outgrow single-node Postgres
Multi-region deployments with follower reads for low-latency agent data access globally
Cassandra-compatible workloads (YCQL) for agents needing wide-column storage
High-availability OLTP with automated failover for critical agent coordination data
Zero-downtime rolling upgrades for always-on agent infrastructure databases
not_for: Simple single-region workloads where standard Postgres is sufficient and cheaper
Teams without DBA expertise for distributed database tuning
best_when: You need Postgres compatibility with horizontal scalability and have outgrown what single-node or read-replica Postgres can provide.
avoid_when: Your workload fits on a single powerful Postgres instance — distributed SQL adds latency and operational complexity without benefit.

auth.notes: Standard Postgres authentication (md5, scram-sha-256, cert). YugabyteDB Managed (cloud) uses certificate-based auth. IAM auth available on AWS/GCP managed deployments.

pricing.free_tier_limits: YugabyteDB Managed: free sandbox cluster (single-region, limited size)
pricing.paid_tiers: Managed: from ~$350/month for 3-node cluster; self-hosted: free OSS; Enterprise license for advanced features
pricing.notes: Core OSS is Apache 2.0 licensed. Enterprise features (encryption at rest, audit logging) require license.

performance.rate_limits: Scales with node count; no hard rate limits

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Postgres-compatible error codes (SQLSTATE); distributed errors may surface as conflict/retry errors
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Full ACID transactions with Postgres semantics; upsert via ON CONFLICT
known_agent_gotchas: Distributed transaction retries required for 40001 (serialization failure) errors — add exponential backoff retry logic
Sequential primary keys cause hotspot on single tablet — use UUID or hash-distributed keys for even load distribution
Follower reads have configurable staleness (default 30s) — agents must tolerate stale data or use leader reads
DDL operations acquire cluster-wide locks — avoid schema changes during high-traffic periods
Postgres extensions vary in support — check YB compatibility table before relying on pgvector or other extensions

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS optional on self-hosted (must be configured). Managed service enforces TLS. Column-level encryption available in enterprise.
